elements.sent-2411-002: Given that the hardware configuration is fixed post-manufacture, proper task mapping attracts researchers' interest.
elements.sent-2411-003: We propose a travel time based task mapping method that allocates uneven counts of tasks across different Processing Elements (PEs).
elements.sent-2411-004: This approach utilizes the travel time recorded in the sampling window and implicitly makes use of static NoC architecture information and dynamic NoC congestion status.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we examine the effectiveness of our method under various configurations, including different mapping iterations, flit sizes, and NoC architectures.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our method achieves up to 12.1\% improvement compared with even mapping and static distance mapping for one layer.
elements.sent-2411-007: For a complete NN example, our method achieves 10.37\% and 13.75\% overall improvements to row-major mapping and distance-based mapping, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-008: While ideal travel time based mapping (post-run) achieves 10.37\% overall improvements to row-major mapping, we adopt a sampling window to efficiently map tasks during the running, achieving 8.17\% (sampling window 10) improvement.
elements.sent-2411-009: Due to the high computational complexity of DNN, hardware acceleration abstracts significant interest among researchers.
elements.sent-2411-010: Furthermore, NoCs have demonstrated remarkable potential in facilitating efficient on-chip data communication for operating neural networks \cite{xue2022aome .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The prevalent method, even mapping in \cite{chen2019eyerissV2 , entails distributing tasks equally across all available Processing Elements (PEs) in a single iteration and repeating this process until task allocation is complete.}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, even mapping does not consider the variance between different PEs, such as the distance to memory, and the run-time status of the NoC, leading to an unbalanced workload and time consumption.
elements.sent-2411-013: Our research aims to enhance the efficiency of accelerators by mapping different numbers of tasks among PEs, due to their inherent differences and varying running status.
elements.sent-2411-014: By allocating fewer tasks to slower PEs, we aim to balance the NoC workload, minimize idle time, and thereby improve overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-015: The contributions of our work are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-016: We propose a travel time based mapping strategy to unevenly distribute tasks to PEs and reduce time consumption by balancing workload in NoC-based DNN accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-017: We investigate the impact of different NN layers, communication protocols, and hardware platforms by analyzing the performance of a layer under various conditions of mapping iterations, packet size, and NoC architecture.
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, we evaluate our method on a complete DNN model, LeNet\cite{lenet .}
elements.sent-2411-019: We propose an on-the-fly travel time based mapping with a sampling window.
elements.sent-2411-020: We discuss the influence of various sampling window lengths.
elements.sent-2411-021: In \sect{Task mapping bg , we introduce the state-of-the-art task mapping methods and then present our travel time based mapping method in \sect{Task mapping method .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In \sect{sectexperiments , we show the experimental results to validate our approach.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Many works focus on non-DNN tasks including Best Neighbor mapping \cite{bestneighbormpeg4 for MPEG-4 and multiwindow display (MWD) tasks, \cite{stuijk2008resource for synthetic traffic and MPEG-4 applications, L-shape isolated (Liso) mapping \cite{sadeghi2019toward for PARSEC and SPLASH-2 benchmarks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The mapping of such applications and benchmarks usually needs to consider diverse task dependencies, which leads to non-regular traffic patterns in NoC.
elements.sent-2411-026: GAMMA \cite{GAMMA introduces a specialized genetic algorithm performing a search in the massive mapping space.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Autonomous Optimal Mapping Exploration (AOME) \cite{xue2022aome leverages two reinforcement learning algorithms to efficiently explore optimal hardware mapping.}
elements.sent-2411-028: ZigZag \cite{zigzaguneven extends the normal design space exploitation by introducing uneven mapping opportunities.}
elements.sent-2411-029: These methodologies, prioritizing the exploration, are time intensive.
elements.sent-2411-030: Similarly, Neu-NoC \cite{neunoc aims to minimize redundant data traffic within neuromorphic acceleration systems and improve data transfer capabilities between adjacent layers.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Configuring their nodes to transmit data to other computation nodes executing adjacent layers instead of directly to memory nodes makes the hardware complex.
elements.sent-2411-032: Their approach focuses on maximizing the reuse of cached data.
elements.sent-2411-033: Work-stealing \cite{blumofe1999scheduling is a dynamic load-balancing strategy that allows idle PEs in the network to actively seek tasks from busier PEs to optimize resource utilization and improve overall performance.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Girao et al. \cite{girao2013exploring explores work-stealing mapping policies to determine whether a task migration between the cores should take place.}
elements.sent-2411-035: It is flexible but frequently collecting the real-time workload status from other PEs is a large overhead for DNN tasks.
elements.sent-2411-036: Additionally, it does not explore the effects of varying configurations on the partitioning.
elements.sent-2411-037: It effectively balances the workload using a simple control algorithm and does not require an extra run.
elements.subsection-2411-001: DNN Tasks and Mapping to NoC
elements.sent-2411-038: We introduce the tasks to be mapped from a DNN model on the left side of \fig{fig:taskmapping_horizonal , using the first layer of LeNet as an example.}
elements.sent-2411-039: This layer processes a 32x32 padded input image through a 5x5 kernel convolution.
elements.sent-2411-040: This convolution operation constitutes a computation task and yields a pixel in the output feature map.
elements.sent-2411-041: While a 4x4 NoC serves as an example, mapping is to allocate tasks to 16 clusters.
elements.sent-2411-042: For example, the first cluster on the right side, highlighted in blue, contains $Count0$ tasks, correspondingly color-coded in blue among all tasks within the cloud-shaped figure.
elements.sent-2411-043: Memory controller (MC) nodes are not assigned any tasks as they contain MCs rather than PEs.
elements.sent-2411-044: Allocating tasks to the entire NoC at once constitutes one mapping iteration.
elements.sent-2411-045: The left side shows $Count_{\text{even \times \text{PENum}$ green tasks, which constitute the main portion, along with the tail tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: These tasks are evenly mapped across the PEs following the row order.
elements.sent-2411-047: To utilize the NoC architecture information, we explore the mapping according to different distances to memory nodes.
elements.sent-2411-048: A node is categorized as "distance 1" if it directly surrounds an MC node, as shown in \fig{fig:distanceMapping .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Similarly, nodes further from the MC are classified as "distance 2" and "distance 3" and colored.
elements.sent-2411-050: Inspired by longer distances leading to longer time consumption, we distribute fewer tasks to far nodes as shown in \equ{distancetaskratio .}
elements.sent-2411-051: The total number of tasks is calculated by multiplying the number of nodes by the task count per node, as shown in \equ{distancetaskall .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Combining \equ{distancetaskratio and \equ{distancetaskall , we can compute the task number for each node and map these tasks accordingly. \begin{equation}Task_{count1}*Distance1 =Task_{count2}*Distance2= Task_{count3}*Distance3 \label{distancetaskratio}\end{equation} \begin{equation}Task_{all} = Num_{D1}*Task_{count1} +Num_{D2}* Task_{count2} + Num_{D3}* Task_{count3} \label{distancetaskall}\end{equation} \par This approach relies on static distance information, leading to fixed ratios despite varying NN model configurations and run-time conditions.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: We explore the use of static latency, post-run recorded latency, and run-time travel time within a sampling window.
elements.sent-2411-054: The first step is to send a request packet from the PE node to the MC node to gather data as the brown path in \fig{fig:travelTimeInNoC .}
elements.sent-2411-055: This packet contains a compact payload such as the source ID and the index of the data needed, comprising only one single flit.
elements.sent-2411-056: We present the time as a light yellow path in \fig{fig:travelTimeInNoC .}
elements.sent-2411-057: By dividing the packet size by the bit count of a single flit, larger kernels generate packets containing more flits, leading to a longer travel time in NoC.
elements.sent-2411-058: The trajectory is tracked from the moment the first flit leaves the MC node's NI until the last flit arrives at the requesting PE's router's VC buffer.
elements.sent-2411-059: This travel path is the purple path in \fig{fig:travelTimeInNoC .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Computation time, the dark green path in \fig{fig:travelTimeInNoC , varies across different layers due to different kernel sizes but is constant in the same layer.}
elements.sent-2411-061: PE will generate the next request packet while previous results are on the way.
elements.sent-2411-062: To highlight the feature of this overlap and avoid counting this overlapped travel time twice, we plot the result packet travel path by a dotted line.
elements.sent-2411-063: We present the travel time used in our proposed mapping method in \equ{traveltimesum .}
elements.sent-2411-064: The NoC congestion status is not directly but implicitly included in $T_{req}$ and $T_{resp}$. \begin{equation}T_{travel} = T_{req} +T_{memaccess}+ T_{resp} + T_{compu} \label{traveltimesum}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: The overall task count is shown in \equ{traveltaskall .}
elements.sent-2411-066: By solving solve \equ{traveltaskall and \equ{traveltimeratio , we compute the task counts for the corresponding task cluster.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Travel time Based mapping shown in \fig{Travel time-based mapping is to allocate task clusters according to different PEs. \begin{equation}\begin{split} Task_{count1}*T_{travel1}=Task_{count2}*T_{travel2}=...= Task_{countN}*T_{travelN} \end{split} \label{traveltimeratio}\end{equation} \begin{equation}Task_{all} =Task_{count1} + Task_{count2} +... +Task_{countN} \label{traveltaskall}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The PE computation time is derived from the workload divided by the available MAC count.
elements.sent-2411-069: The memory access time also depends on the workload and the bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-070: By dividing the data size by bandwidth, we can compute the delay caused by memory access.
elements.sent-2411-071: Estimating time spent in NoC is challenging, but it will at least equal the product of distance and link delay.
elements.sent-2411-072: If multiple flits are involved, the latency between the head and tail flits must also be considered.
elements.sent-2411-073: Additionally, there are fixed overheads such as packetization latency. \begin{equation}T_{SL} = T_{compu}+T_{memaccess} +(D* T\_{link}+ (FlitNum-1)*T_{flit} )+ T_{fixed} \label{equationstaticlatency}\end{equation} \par.
elements.sent-2411-074: Travel time is precisely recorded during a complete run, and mapping is performed afterward.
elements.sent-2411-075: This method yields accurate travel time data for use in \equ{traveltimeratio , but necessitates an additional run.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Sampling Window in Travel Time Based Mapping
elements.sent-2411-076: We divide the mapping process into two different routes in \fig{fig:sampingWindowFlowChart.png2 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: The left route is for a small layer without enough samples and a row-major mapping is directly used.
elements.sent-2411-078: For a layer with enough tasks, we employ a short sampling window to estimate travel times rather than recording accurate time after one complete run.
elements.sent-2411-079: The sampling window length, $SamplingWindowLength$, determines the sampled task number for one PE.
elements.sent-2411-080: After sampling, we recorded travel time and computed the task number to be allocated for each node.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width = 8 cm]{figures/SystemDesign/sampingWindowFlowChart2.png} \caption{Flowchart of travel time based task mapping with sampling window} \label{fig:sampingWindowFlowChart.png2}
elements.sent-2411-081: Additionally, the scope of task mapping shifts from encompassing all tasks to focusing on the residual tasks, as delineated in \equ{taskall-penum=taskformapping .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Combining \equ{traveltimeratiosampled and \equ{taskall-penum=taskformapping , we can compute the task number allocated to each PE after sampling. \begin{equation}Task_{count1}*T_{s1} = Task_{count2}*T_{s2} = ...= Task_{countN}*T_{sN} \label{traveltimeratiosampled}\end{equation} \begin{equation}(Task_{all}- Task_{sampled}) =Task_{count1} + Task_{count2} +... +Task_{countN} \label{taskall-penum=taskformapping}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: We abstract the difference between these three parts and configure three variables separately: mapping iteration, packet size, and NoC hardware architecture.
elements.sent-2411-084: Mapping iterations/packet number: determined by different DNN input feature map sizes, DNN output channels, and DNN kernel sizes.
elements.sent-2411-085: Packet size/flit number: determined by DNN kernel size and communication protocol which specifies the bits utilized by a single flit.
elements.sent-2411-086: NoC architecture: PE and MC nodes' varying numbers and positions.
elements.sent-2411-087: First of all, we introduce the simulation platform, a cycle-accurate CNN-NoC accelerator simulation environment based on a behavior-level NoC simulator as outlined in \cite{wang2021flexible .}
elements.sent-2411-088: The NoC consists of a VC network derived from Gem5-Garnet \cite{agarwal2009garnet , utilizing the widely used X-Y routing algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Each physical connection between routers comprises four virtual channels, with each VC containing a four-flit buffer.
elements.sent-2411-090: The NoC's operating frequency is set at 2 GHz, following the specifications in \cite{wang2021flexible , while the PEs are configured to function at 200 MHz, as detailed in \cite{hu2022high .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: These configurations were selected due to their widespread acceptance in the field.
elements.sent-2411-092: Each PE contains 64 Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) units, mirroring the 64 MACs per PE configuration in the Simba \cite{shao2019simba architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-093: The computation delay is calculated by dividing the required MAC operations by 64.
elements.sent-2411-094: For example, a convolution requiring 25 MAC operations consumes one PE cycle and 128 MAC operations consume 2 PE cycles.
elements.sent-2411-095: The two nodes connected to MC have a memory bandwidth of 64 GB/s, emulating a DDR5 device \cite{neda2023ciflow .}
elements.sent-2411-096: One data of weight or input is one 16-bit fixed point number which is two Bytes.
elements.sent-2411-097: Hence, one data consumes $\frac{1 {32*10^9 s=0.0625$ router cycles, and the memory access delay is determined by the data number.. \par.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: To investigate the impact of task count variations, we extend the task count with ratios from 0.5x to 8x by adjusting the output channel from 3 to 48, while the default configuration is 6 (1x).
elements.sent-2411-099: The first layer of LeNet contains an output of 6x28x28 corresponding to 4704 convolution tasks.
elements.sent-2411-100: This means 336 mapping iterations for even mapping due to 14 PE nodes.
elements.sent-2411-101: We explore a range of 2352 to 37632 tasks which means 168 to 2688 mapping iterations.
elements.sent-2411-102: As only the response packet contains data picked up from MC nodes, the request and result packets' sizes are not impacted.
elements.sent-2411-103: We modify the kernel dimensions from 1x1 to 13x13 in \tab{Different kernel size and packet size , including the original 5x5 kernel in the LeNet first layer.}
elements.figure-2411-007: [] \caption{Different kernel size and packet size} \label{Different kernel size and packet size} \begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|c|l|} Input Kernel size Padding \multicolumn{1}{l|}{Mapping iterations} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}Packet size in flits\end{tabular} \multirow{6}{*}{28x28} 1x1 0 \multirow{6}{*}{336} 1 3x3 1 2 5x5 (original) 2 4 7x7 3 7 9x9 4 11 11x11 5 16 13x13 6 22 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-104: Our investigation also extends to two different NoC architectures: one with two MCs and the other with four MCs.
elements.sent-2411-105: Unless otherwise specified, we use a sampling window of 10 in travel time-based DNN task mapping.
elements.sent-2411-106: We introduce the unevenness between task complementing time in \equ{eq1 .}
elements.sent-2411-107: The unevenness $\rho$ is the difference between the maximum delay by the slowest node and the minimum latency by the fastest node divided by the maximum latency.
elements.sent-2411-108: Our analysis focuses on minimizing the maximum time consumption because it, rather than average time overhead, determines the final inference time for a layer. \begin{equation}\label{eq1} \rho = \frac{T_{max}-T_{min}}{T_{max}}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-109: To present the results more effectively, we arrange the results of the 14 PE nodes in an order of increasing distances.
elements.sent-2411-110: The end-to-end completion time for one DNN task ranges from 57.69 to 77.88 cycles, showing an unevenness of 25.92\%.

elements.sent-2411-111: Within the same PE, a response packet is always generated after the request packet.
elements.sent-2411-112: Although packets from different PEs may interleave within the network, they are processed sequentially within their respective PEs.
elements.sent-2411-113: Consequently, we depict them stacked in Figure \ref{unevenxmappingsum.png .}
elements.sent-2411-114: The unevenness in the partially accumulated time consumption is 22.09\%, showing that the fastest PE—represented by the lowest bar in \fig{unevenxmappingsum.png —is idle for 22.09\% time in this layer while the slowest PE is continuing its operation.}
elements.sent-2411-115: This scenario highlights the inefficiency in resource usage and underscores the necessity for uneven mapping strategies.
elements.sent-2411-116: Nodes 13, 5, and 8 are the fastest as they have only one distance, and nodes 1, 4, and 12 consume moderate time as their distances are two.
elements.sent-2411-117: Node 0 has the longest distance, three, and requires the most time.
elements.sent-2411-118: However, simply allocating the task following the ratio of distance, leads to the unevenness of 58.03\% in \fig{unevendistancesum.png .}
elements.sent-2411-119: This reveals that we need a better mapping method to set the ratio rather than simply adopting the distances as ratios.
elements.sent-2411-120: By allocating a variable number of tasks to each PE, we nearly equalize the total time consumption for all packets per PE in \fig{uneventravelsum.png and \fig{postuneventravelsum.png .}}
elements.sent-2411-121: We effectively balance overall time consumption on each PE and achieve an unevenness of 5.81\% and 6.24\%.
elements.sent-2411-122: To better compare the time consumption across different mapping methods, we show the percentage difference of each bar to the orange bar, which is the slowest PE in row-major mapping.
elements.sent-2411-123: For row-major mapping, under all mapping iterations, there is a gap of around 21\% and this shows potential to improve as one PE is idle while the other PE is working.
elements.sent-2411-124: In distance-based mapping, the fastest PE operates for a shorter duration compared to its counterpart in row-major mapping, while the slowest PE works for a longer period.
elements.sent-2411-125: This outcome illustrates the challenge and complexity of achieving effective uneven mapping.
elements.sent-2411-126: Compared with row-major mapping, the total time consumed for processing a single layer is dominated by the slowest PE, and our method achieves notable improvement around 9.7\%.
elements.sent-2411-127: All distance-based mapping worsens the situation by increasing the unevenness together with the final latency dominated by the slowest PE.
elements.sent-2411-128: Static-latency-based mapping achieves high performance while the flit number is small.
elements.sent-2411-129: However, as the number of flits increases, more flits are injected into the NoC, and the exclusion of congestion and queuing delays in the static latency calculations leads to decreased performance.
elements.sent-2411-130: Our proposed method enhances the latency for one single layer, reducing the time consumption of one layer up to 12.1\%.
elements.sent-2411-131: This demonstrates the complexity of setting the ratio for uneven mapping and validates the effectiveness of our proposed method.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[trim=2cm 0pt 2cm 2cm, clip, width = 12 cm]{figures/experimentResults/flitnumbers/flitnumberresults.png} \caption{Inference time for one layer with varying kernel size and packet size} \label{fig:flitnumberresults}
elements.sent-2411-132: Compared to row-major mapping, the enhancement provided by our method in configurations with four MC nodes is 5.6\%, which is less than the 9.5\% improvement observed in the default settings.
elements.sent-2411-133: This reduced enhancement is caused by the minimal variance in distances among nodes, which lowers the gap between the fastest PE and the slowest PE in row-major mapping from 21.7\% to 9.3\%, resulting in a narrower space for optimization.

elements.subsection-2411-011: Experiment Results of Mapping Whole NN Model with Different Sampling Windows
elements.sent-2411-134: Within each cluster, six distinct inference times are documented: those resulting from row-major mapping, distance-based mapping, and travel time based mapping with sampling windows of 1, 5, and 10, alongside the post-run travel time based mapping.
elements.sent-2411-135: The percentage improvements over row-major mapping, for each cluster, are also presented in polylines, offering a comprehensive comparison of each mapping strategy under varying conditions.
elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{adjustwidth}{-1cm}{-1cm} \includegraphics[width = 12 cm]{figures/experimentResults/leNetResultsmemnodes2.png} \caption{Inference time for LeNet} \label{fig:Inference time for LeNet} \end{adjustwidth}
elements.sent-2411-136: Static latency based mapping achieves a performance level that is intermediate between travel time based mapping of long and short sampling windows.
elements.sent-2411-137: For a sampling window of 1, performance drops are observed in layers 3, 5, and 6 due to the extremely few sampling packets.
elements.sent-2411-138: With a sampling window of 5, the performance is improved and only layer 6 shows a decrease compared to row-major mapping.
elements.sent-2411-139: It may be caused by the small packet count of 84 in layer 6; the minor loss is just 105 cycles.
elements.sent-2411-140: With a longer sampling window of 10, the performance no longer worsens compared to row-major mapping in any layer.
elements.sent-2411-141: All travel time based mappings show improvement over row-major mapping and over distance-based mapping for the whole time consumption.
elements.sent-2411-142: With an increased sampling window length from 1 to 10, the overall improvement increases from 1.78\% to 8.17\%, approaching the ideal post-run travel time based mapping of 10.37\%.
elements.sent-2411-143: Unevenness exists in even mapping, across various configurations including mapping iterations, packet size, and NoC architecture.
elements.sent-2411-144: Our proposed mapping consistently outperforms both row-major and distance-based mapping by dynamically determining the task ratio according to the travel time.
elements.sent-2411-145: A post-run travel time based mapping invariably offers the best performance in all layers and the whole model, compared with row-major mapping, distance-based mapping, and travel time based mapping with a sampling window.
elements.sent-2411-146: However, it needs one additional run to collect accurate travel time, resulting in extra time and energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-147: Utilizing a sampling window during operation time provides sampled rather than precise travel times for mapping, eliminating the need for an extra run.
elements.sent-2411-148: Larger windows tend to offer better approximations as more samples are recorded.
elements.sent-2411-149: A sampling window of 10 offers an effective trade-off between performance and sample number, achieving an 8.17\% improvement for a whole NN model, closely approaching the ideal post-run gain of 10.37\%.
elements.sent-2411-150: For a window length of 1, the travel time has a bias compared to the accurate travel time in post-run, leading to varying final delay improvement for the whole LeNet compared with row-major mapping, from 1.78\% for window 1 and 10.37\% for post-run.
elements.sent-2411-151: This indicates that a too-small sample size is ineffective.
elements.sent-2411-152: Compared to the 10.37\% improvement achieved by the ideal post-run outcomes, our implementation of a 10-task sampling window achieves an 8.17\% improvement over row-major mapping.
elements.sent-2411-153: This high effectiveness is due to the repetitive nature of NN tasks, which makes network activities similarly predictable and minimizes the need for extensive sampling.
elements.sent-2411-154: We compare our approach with traditional even mapping and distance-based mapping strategies.
elements.sent-2411-155: The results indicate that relying solely on the distance information is inadequate, thereby validating the significance of our method.
elements.sent-2411-156: Additionally, we examine the effects of different mapping iterations, packet sizes, and NoC architectures—factors corresponding to varying neural network models, communication protocols, and hardware platforms.
elements.sent-2411-157: Beyond collecting information from extra running, we proposed a technique for sampling travel time information during runtime and evaluated the impact of different window lengths.
elements.sent-2411-158: Consequently, we achieved a maximum 9.7\% improvement in latency for varying mapping iterations, 12.1\% for different packet sizes, and 9.7\% for diverse NoC architectures.
elements.sent-2411-159: With post-run travel time based mapping, our method obtains an enhancement of 10.37\% for the whole LeNet.
elements.sent-2411-160: By implementing dynamic window lengths, we were able to estimate improvements without additional simulations, achieving 1.78\%, 6.62\%, and 8.17\% improvements for sampling window lengths of 1, 5, and 10, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-161: This reveals that using more samples achieves a performance more similar to the ideal result with post-run.
elements.sent-2411-162: In addition, since wiring and thermal management will directly affect the NoC performance, their impact when applying our solution is worth investigating.. \bibliographystyle{splncs04.}
